content.problemStatement:
In ACM-ICPC contests, there are usually three people in a team. For each person in the team, you know their scores in three skills - hard work, intelligence and persistence.

You want to check whether it is possible to order these people (assign them numbers from 1 to 3) in such a way that for each **1 ≤ i ≤ 2**, **i+1**-th person is stricly better than the **i**-th person.

A person **x** is said to be better than another person **y** if **x** doesn't score less than **y** in any of the skills and scores more than **y** in at least one skill.

Determine whether such an ordering exists.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T denoting the number of test cases. Each test case consists of three lines, each containing three space-separated integers s1, s2, and s3, representing the scores of one team member in three skills.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing 'yes' if an ordering exists or 'no' if it doesn't exist.

sampleTestCases.explanation: We can order them as (3, 2, 1). Person 3 is better than Person 2 because his scores in the first two skills are not lesser than Person 2's. And in skill 3, Person 3 scores higher. Similarly, Person 2 is better than Person 1. He scores more than Person 1 in every skill, in fact.
There is no ordering where each subsequent person is better than the previous one.

We can order them as (1, 2, 3). Person 2 is better than Person 1 because his scores in the first two skills are not lesser than Person 1's. And in skill 3, Person 2 scores higher. Similarly, Person 3 is better than Person 2. He scores more than Person 2 in every skill, in fact.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we need to compare the skills of each team member and determine if an ordering exists where each subsequent member is better than the previous one. We can achieve this by sorting the team members based on their skills and then checking if the sorted order satisfies the condition.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * 3!) — because for each test case, we generate all permutations of the team members and check if any permutation satisfies the condition.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — because we only use a constant amount of space to store the team members and their skills.
